content.text: Traditional user stories describe what a user wants a system to do. When the system is a conversational AI or an agentic workflow, the story format must capture not only what the user wants to accomplish but also the nature of the conversational exchange that accomplishes it — whether the task resolves in one turn, requires iterative dialogue, or involves an agent taking autonomous action across multiple tools. Job stories, which frame requirements as situational motivations rather than feature requests, provide the natural extension point for capturing these conversational dynamics.
What It Is

Job stories are a requirements format derived from the Jobs-to-be-Done (JTBD) theory introduced by Clayton Christensen and popularised in product development by Alan Klement. Where a user story frames a requirement from the perspective of a user role ("As a [user], I want [feature], so that [benefit]"), a job story frames it from the perspective of a situation ("When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [expected outcome]"). The situational framing is more precise because it grounds the requirement in the specific context that triggers the job, which makes it more testable and more resistant to feature creep.

For conversational AI and agentic applications, job stories are extended with a fourth clause: the WITHOUT clause. This clause captures the AI-specific constraint that must be satisfied — "without having to phrase my question in a specific way," "without losing the context from earlier in the conversation," "without needing to approve each individual API call." The WITHOUT clause is what differentiates a job story designed for an AI feature from one designed for a conventional software feature: it makes the AI's specific responsibilities explicit rather than leaving them implied.

Conversational job stories classify into three types based on the nature of the AI interaction they describe. Single-turn stories describe tasks that are resolved in one exchange — the user provides a complete prompt and the AI provides a complete response. Multi-turn stories describe tasks that require iterative dialogue over multiple exchanges, where each turn builds on the context of the previous ones. Agentic stories describe tasks where the AI takes autonomous action across external tools or systems, making multiple decisions and API calls on the user's behalf to accomplish a goal.
Why It Matters

User stories for AI features are frequently too abstract to be directly implementable. "As a user, I want the AI to help me write emails" could describe anything from a single-turn composition feature to a full email management agent that reads, prioritises, drafts, and sends on the user's behalf. The gap between these two interpretations is enormous in implementation complexity, data access requirements, and risk profile. Job stories with the WITHOUT clause force product and engineering to agree on which interpretation is intended before a story is scheduled.

The situational framing also improves evaluation test case quality. Because a job story specifies the situation that triggers the job, the evaluation suite can be built around realistic triggering situations rather than synthetic test inputs that may not reflect actual usage patterns. A job story that begins "When I have received a meeting request from a client I am unfamiliar with" naturally suggests evaluation test cases that use realistic calendar and contact data rather than placeholder inputs.
Benefits
Situational precision
— the WHEN clause anchors the requirement in a specific context, preventing stories that are too broad to test or implement reliably.
AI responsibility clarity
— the WITHOUT clause makes explicit what the AI must handle without requiring the user to do it, capturing the AI's specific value contribution in the story.
Conversation type classification

— classifying stories as single-turn, multi-turn, or agentic surfaces architectural implications early: multi-turn stories require session state; agentic stories require tool execution and approval flows.
Evaluation test case seeds
— the four clauses of the job story (WHEN, WANT, SO I CAN, WITHOUT) map directly to the input, task, success criterion, and constraint of an LLM evaluation test case.
Resistance to scope creep
— the situational framing makes it harder to add implicit features to a story, since any addition must also specify a situation and a WITHOUT constraint.
Trade-offs and Limitations

Job stories are harder to write than user stories and require more product thinking — teams unfamiliar with JTBD theory often default to the user story format even when job stories would be more appropriate.
The classification into single-turn, multi-turn, and agentic requires technical knowledge that product managers may not have, creating dependency on engineering input during story writing.

Agentic job stories can be difficult to validate before the agent is built — the WITHOUT clause for an agentic story often depends on technical decisions (which tools the agent can call) that are made during implementation rather than discovery.

The format does not directly capture non-functional requirements like latency, accuracy thresholds, or availability — these must be added as explicit acceptance criteria on the story rather than inferred from the four clauses.
How It Works

Writing a conversational job story involves four steps: identify the triggering situation, articulate the user's motivation in that situation, define the expected outcome, and write the WITHOUT clause that captures what the AI must handle autonomously or transparently. The resulting four-clause story is then classified by conversation type and enriched with acceptance criteria derived from the evaluation strategy.
Writing the WHEN Clause

The WHEN clause describes the specific situation that triggers the need for the AI. It should be concrete enough to distinguish this situation from related but distinct situations. "When I need help with writing" is too broad — it could apply to dozens of different jobs. "When I have drafted a technical specification that needs to be adapted for a non-technical executive audience" is specific enough to imply a clear AI task, an appropriate response format, and a testable outcome.
Writing the I WANT TO Clause

The I WANT TO clause captures the user's motivation in the situation — what they are trying to accomplish, not what feature they want. "I want to click a 'simplify' button" is a feature request. "I want to quickly produce a version of this document that any non-technical reader can understand" is a motivation. The distinction matters because the motivation remains stable as the implementation evolves, while feature requests may become obsolete.
Writing the SO I CAN Clause

The SO I CAN clause identifies the higher-order outcome the user is pursuing beyond the immediate task. This clause prevents implementation that technically satisfies the job but misses the point — an AI that rewrites a technical document at a 10th-grade reading level technically simplifies it, but if the user's goal is to impress an executive, the tone and structure also matter. The SO I CAN clause surfaces the success criterion that the AI output must meet.
Writing the WITHOUT Clause

The WITHOUT clause captures the constraint that the AI must satisfy to make the job worth doing. For single-turn stories, this typically describes input tolerance ("without having to provide a detailed template") or output quality ("without producing a version that reads as AI-generated"). For multi-turn stories, it typically describes state management ("without needing to re-explain the context each time"). For agentic stories, it typically describes autonomy requirements ("without approving each individual calendar check and email draft") and safety constraints ("without sending anything before showing me a preview").
Architecture

attrs.alt: Job story structure with three conversation types for AI tasks

content.text: The diagram shows the four-clause job story structure (WHEN, I WANT TO, SO I CAN, WITHOUT) applied to three conversation types: single-turn (one exchange resolves the job), multi-turn (dialogue over multiple exchanges), and agentic (agent takes autonomous action across tools). Each type maps to a distinct architecture: single prompt and response, stateful conversation thread, or multi-step agent plan with tool calls.
When to Use It

Job stories are the preferred requirements format for any AI feature where the conversational exchange structure is a first-class design concern — that is, any feature where the number of turns, the statefulness of the conversation, or the agent's autonomy level affects the user experience. This includes all conversational AI assistants, all multi-step agentic workflows, and any feature where the AI's handling of follow-up questions or contextual references is part of the core user expectation.

User stories remain appropriate for AI features where the conversational structure is trivial (the AI does one thing in response to one input and there is no follow-up) and where the primary design concern is the quality of the output rather than the nature of the interaction.
How to Implement
Classify the job first

: Before writing the story, determine whether the job is single-turn, multi-turn, or agentic. This classification determines the technical architecture required and the WITHOUT clause type to focus on.
Write the WHEN clause from real user situations
: Use situations drawn from user research, support tickets, or observation sessions rather than hypothetical scenarios. Real situations produce better WHEN clauses.
Keep the I WANT TO clause motivation-level
: Review each clause and ask "is this what the user wants to do, or what they want the system to do?" If it describes a system behaviour, it belongs in the WITHOUT clause, not the I WANT TO clause.
Write the WITHOUT clause for every AI feature

: Do not skip this clause. Even simple single-turn features have implicit WITHOUT clauses that should be made explicit — "without having to rephrase the request" and "without receiving a response that requires specialist knowledge to interpret" are both valid and useful.
Derive evaluation test cases from the story
: Map each clause to an evaluation dimension: WHEN → test input context, I WANT TO → task specification, SO I CAN → success criterion, WITHOUT → constraint that must not be violated.
Add acceptance criteria for non-functional requirements
: Append explicit ACs for latency, accuracy threshold, and hallucination rate — these are not captured by the four job story clauses but are essential for AI features.
Examples
The following Python code implements a job story structure with classification, validation, and automatic evaluation scenario generation:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class StoryType(Enum):
    SINGLE_TURN = "single_turn"
    MULTI_TURN = "multi_turn"
    AGENTIC = "agentic"

@dataclass
class AcceptanceCriterion:
    description: str
    testable: bool
    metric: Optional[str] = None  # e.g. "p95 < 2000ms", "accuracy >= 0.95"

@dataclass
class ConversationalJobStory:
    id: str
    persona: str
    story_type: StoryType

    # Four clauses
    when: str        # situational trigger
    i_want_to: str   # user motivation
    so_i_can: str    # expected outcome
    without: str     # AI-specific constraint

    # Additional metadata
    acceptance_criteria: list[AcceptanceCriterion] = field(default_factory=list)

    def validate(self) -> list[str]:
        """Check the story for common problems."""
        issues = []
        if "want to click" in self.i_want_to.lower() or "want to see" in self.i_want_to.lower():
            issues.append("I WANT TO clause describes a UI action, not a motivation")
        if len(self.when.split()) < 5:
            issues.append("WHEN clause too vague — add more situational context")
        if self.without == "":
            issues.append("WITHOUT clause missing — AI-specific constraint not captured")
        if not self.acceptance_criteria:
            issues.append("No acceptance criteria — add latency and accuracy ACs")
        return issues

    def to_eval_scenario(self) -> dict:
        """Convert story to an evaluation test case template."""
        return {
            "story_id": self.id,
            "story_type": self.story_type.value,
            "input_context": self.when,
            "task": self.i_want_to,
            "success_criterion": self.so_i_can,
            "constraint": self.without,
            "persona": self.persona,
            "test_inputs": [],  # to be populated with real examples
            "pass_condition": self.so_i_can,
            "fail_conditions": [self.without],
        }

# Examples across all three story types
stories = [
    ConversationalJobStory(
        id="S001",
        persona="Morgan Reyes (Junior Associate)",
        story_type=StoryType.SINGLE_TURN,
        when="I have found a case and need to quickly determine if it is relevant to my matter",
        i_want_to="get a one-paragraph summary of the case's holding, key facts, and jurisdiction",
        so_i_can="decide in under 30 seconds whether to read the full opinion",
        without="having to read the 40-page opinion first or provide any context beyond the case name",
        acceptance_criteria=[
            AcceptanceCriterion("Summary includes holding, key facts, and jurisdiction", True),
            AcceptanceCriterion("Response latency", True, metric="p95 < 3000ms"),
            AcceptanceCriterion("Accuracy of holding summary", True, metric=">= 0.95 on golden dataset"),
            AcceptanceCriterion("No hallucinated citations in summary", True, metric="0 fabricated citations"),
        ],
    ),
    ConversationalJobStory(
        id="S002",
        persona="Morgan Reyes (Junior Associate)",
        story_type=StoryType.MULTI_TURN,
        when="I am drafting a research memo and need to explore how several cases interact",
        i_want_to="ask follow-up questions about each case without re-explaining the memo context",
        so_i_can="build a coherent analysis across multiple cases in a single session",
        without="losing the thread of which cases I have already discussed or why I am asking",
        acceptance_criteria=[
            AcceptanceCriterion("Session state maintained across at least 10 turns", True),
            AcceptanceCriterion("Case references in later turns correctly linked to earlier turns", True),
            AcceptanceCriterion("Session context not lost on network interruption (auto-save)", True),
        ],
    ),
    ConversationalJobStory(
        id="S003",
        persona="Jordan Kim (Compliance Officer)",
        story_type=StoryType.AGENTIC,
        when="I need to check whether a new vendor contract contains any clauses that violate our standard policy",
        i_want_to="have the AI read the full contract and flag every policy-violating clause with the relevant policy reference",
        so_i_can="review only the flagged clauses rather than reading the entire contract",
        without="needing to paste each clause manually or approve the AI reading each section individually",
        acceptance_criteria=[
            AcceptanceCriterion("All policy-violating clauses identified (recall >= 0.98)", True, metric="recall >= 0.98"),
            AcceptanceCriterion("False positive rate below 5%", True, metric="precision >= 0.95"),
            AcceptanceCriterion("No contract content sent outside approved AI infrastructure", True),
            AcceptanceCriterion("Human review required before any remediation is suggested to vendor", True),
        ],
    ),
]

for story in stories:
    issues = story.validate()
    print(f"[{story.id}] {story.story_type.value.upper()}")
    print(f"  WHEN: {story.when[:70]}...")
    print(f"  WITHOUT: {story.without[:70]}...")
    if issues:
        for issue in issues:
            print(f"  ! {issue}")
    else:
        print(f"  ✓ Valid")
    print()
A second example shows the mapping from job story clauses to evaluation test case format:

import json
from typing import Any

def generate_eval_suite(stories: list[ConversationalJobStory]) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """
    Generate a skeletal evaluation suite from a list of job stories.
    Each story becomes a test group; each AC becomes a check.
    """
    suite = {"version": "1.0", "test_groups": []}

    for story in stories:
        checks = []
        for ac in story.acceptance_criteria:
            checks.append({
                "description": ac.description,
                "metric": ac.metric,
                "testable": ac.testable,
                "source": f"AC on story {story.id}",
            })

        # Add implicit checks from WITHOUT clause
        checks.append({
            "description": f"WITHOUT violation check: {story.without}",
            "metric": None,
            "testable": True,
            "source": f"WITHOUT clause on story {story.id}",
        })

        suite["test_groups"].append({
            "story_id": story.id,
            "story_type": story.story_type.value,
            "persona": story.persona,
            "situation": story.when,
            "task": story.i_want_to,
            "success_criterion": story.so_i_can,
            "checks": checks,
            "test_inputs": []  # populated during test-data collection sprint
        })

    return suite

suite = generate_eval_suite(stories)
print(json.dumps(suite, indent=2)[:800], "...")
Real-World Example

An enterprise knowledge management company building an AI assistant for technical support engineers used job stories to specify their conversational features. Through a combination of user research and support ticket analysis, they identified 22 distinct jobs-to-be-done for the AI assistant. Classifying these by story type revealed that 12 were single-turn (one-shot lookups of documentation, procedures, or troubleshooting steps), 7 were multi-turn (iterative troubleshooting conversations where the engineer needed to share device logs and receive step-by-step guidance), and 3 were agentic (automated escalation flows where the AI would check ticket history, create a priority escalation, and notify the on-call engineer).

This classification drove three distinct architectural decisions: the single-turn stories required only a RAG pipeline with a knowledge base; the multi-turn stories required a persistent session state store and a context management layer; the agentic stories required a tool integration layer with explicit human approval for escalation actions. Without the job story classification, the team had initially assumed all 22 jobs could be served by the same simple RAG implementation. The classification process alone prevented a significant architectural refactor six months into development.
Common Mistakes
Writing I WANT TO clauses that describe features rather than motivations
— "I want to use the AI summarisation button" is not a job story; it is a feature request. The clause should describe what the user is trying to accomplish, not how they want to accomplish it.
Writing WHEN clauses that are too broad
— "When I am using the product" or "When I need help" could apply to any feature. The WHEN clause must be specific enough to imply a distinct, testable user situation.
Omitting the WITHOUT clause
— this is the clause most likely to be skipped under time pressure, but it is the clause most specific to AI features. Skipping it leaves the AI's specific responsibilities undefined.
Classifying all stories as single-turn by default
— teams new to conversational AI underestimate the prevalence of multi-turn and agentic jobs. Actively look for stories where the job cannot be done in a single exchange.
Not linking stories to evaluation test cases
— job stories that are written but not converted to evaluation scenarios do not improve the AI feature's quality. The conversion must be an explicit step in the process.
Best Practices
Hold a job story writing workshop that includes both product and engineering participants — the classification step requires technical judgment that product alone may not have.

Maintain a job story registry that tracks each story's type, persona, evaluation coverage status, and the sprint in which it was implemented — this creates a living requirements artefact that can be referenced for scope disputes during implementation.

Write failure job stories alongside success stories: "When the AI provides an incorrect answer, I want to report the error without losing my current context, so I can continue my work without interruption, without having to restart the session." These drive error handling UX requirements.

Use the WITHOUT clause as a pre-commit checklist item: before merging any AI feature code, verify that the WITHOUT constraint is enforced by the implementation and covered by at least one evaluation test.
Performance and Scalability

Job story classification has a direct impact on the scalability of the AI feature architecture. Agentic stories require tool execution infrastructure that must scale differently from conversational context stores for multi-turn stories. Identifying the proportion of each story type early in discovery allows the architecture to be sized correctly from the start rather than being refactored after the feature ships.

The evaluation suite derived from job stories scales linearly with the number of stories and acceptance criteria. A product with 50 job stories and 3 ACs each requires 150 evaluation checks per model version — a manageable set for automated evaluation. Teams that skip job story writing and instead create evaluation test cases ad hoc tend to accumulate duplicates, miss entire job categories, and end up with evaluation suites that are simultaneously too large (many redundant tests) and too narrow (missing whole job types).

Security and Reliability

The WITHOUT clause is the primary vehicle for capturing security constraints in job stories. For any job that involves the AI accessing, processing, or transmitting user data, the WITHOUT clause should include the data handling constraint: "without sending my message history to any third party," "without storing conversation content beyond the session," "without including identifying information in the prompt sent to the model." These constraints, derived from user research and compliance requirements, become testable security requirements.

For agentic job stories, reliability constraints belong in both the WITHOUT clause and the acceptance criteria. The WITHOUT clause captures the user's expectation of autonomy boundaries ("without taking irreversible actions without my explicit confirmation"); the acceptance criteria capture the quantitative reliability thresholds ("the agent must succeed on at least 98% of inputs without human intervention"). Together, these two sources provide a complete reliability specification for the agentic workflow.
Key Takeaways
Job stories extend the standard WHEN/I WANT TO/SO I CAN format with a WITHOUT clause that captures the AI-specific constraint — the most important addition for AI feature requirements.
Classifying stories as single-turn, multi-turn, or agentic surfaces architectural implications early: multi-turn requires state; agentic requires tool execution and approval flows.
The four clauses map directly to evaluation test case components: WHEN → input context, I WANT TO → task, SO I CAN → success criterion, WITHOUT → constraint that must not be violated.
The WITHOUT clause is the clause most likely to be skipped under time pressure, but it is the most AI-specific and therefore the most valuable — make it mandatory.
Story classification drives architecture: a product with a mix of single-turn, multi-turn, and agentic stories requires multiple architectural layers, not a single unified conversational interface.
Job stories version-controlled as code alongside the evaluation suite create a single source of truth linking product requirements to quality verification.
